The Problem of Prediction

During the 20th century knowledge that variations in organisms are heritable led to the development of transmission genetics and to the widespread acceptance among biologists that important aspects of cell and organismic behavior are heritable, reproducible, and ordered. Between the 1950's and 1980's, most biologists came to accept a DNA-centric view that biological phenomena depend on the reading-out of a biological program stored in the genome, i.e., genome products are synthesized and interact with one another and the external environment. Not all behavior is genetically determined: fluctuations in bacteriophage burst size (Delbruck, 1945) and behavior of chemotactic bacteria (Spudich & Koshland, 1976) were attributed to stochastic variations in the small numbers of regulatory molecules. However, everything we know of biology suggests that much of the output of biological regulatory networks, and much of the future behavior of cells and organisms, is determined and determinable from their present states.

Still, the ability of biologists to predict the future behavior of studied systems remains poor. Not only are biologists unable to predict the appearance and behavior of an organism from the sequence of its genome, but usually, they cannot precisely predict the behavior of cells and organisms in response to defined genomic or environmental changes. We can illustrate this poor predictive ability by making an analogy to another science, physics (in this case, mechanics), where prediction is easier. Consider a satellite in orbit around the Earth. For this satellite, knowledge of its position and velocity vector at any point in time allows accurate prediction of its future orbit, and knowledge of the change in the velocity vector at any time point allows accurate prediction of the resulting change in orbit. This predictive power rests on two foundations: the ability to measure the correct quantities (i.e., the satellite's position and velocity vector, but not it's mass or the rate of rotation of the earth) and possession of an analytical framework (i.e., calculus) that defines both the important measurements and the operations on them.

In this analysis, present-day biology still lacks both the ability to measure the right quantities, and it lacks the quantitative analytical framework that defines the relevant measurements and operations needed to make predictions. However, the past decades have seen a good deal of progress in biological understanding resulting from hypothesis driven exploration and from genomic research. For some well-characterized intracellular processes the depth of knowledge may be sufficient to outline the remaining facts that need to be learned in order to describe their future behavior. Similarly, since the 1940s, there has been a great deal of progress in generating computational and analytical frameworks that can work with myriads of important measurements and perform predictive mathematical operations on them.

The premise of the Alpha Project is that progress during the 20th century has laid sufficient groundwork so that, during the next five years, a focused project can make progress towards better observing, computing, and predicting the function of a prototypical eukaryotic regulatory pathway. Success of this work would be directly applicable to the study of biological regulatory networks in other organisms, including humans. It would also increase our ability to identify key intervention points in these networks, facilitating therapy and drug discovery. Furthermore, the ability to predict biological system behavior is essential for engineering biological systems.
